brief:
The single most important signal from this week's data is not what moved — it is what did not. Across every tenor from 91-day bills to 20-year bonds, yields registered exactly **zero basis points** of change, painting a picture of a market that has effectively frozen in place, waiting for a catalyst that has not yet arrived. This eerie calm masks a curve that is anything but normal. The front end is anchored by a 91-day bill at **9.85%**, sitting just **15 basis points** below the repo rate of **10%**, while the 182-day trades at **10.20%** — actually above the policy rate — and then the 364-day dips back to **10.07%**, creating a visible kink. But the real anomaly lives further out: the 2-year bond at **11.95%** is trading **80 basis points** above the 5-year at **10.82%** and a full **80 basis points** above the 10-year at **11.15%**. This is not a normal curve; this is a market screaming that something in the 2-year refinancing pipeline is deeply mispriced or that a pocket of illiquidity has been left to fester. The 15-year at **12.68%** overshooting the 20-year at **11.65%** by over **100 basis points** tells the same story of segmented demand rather than smooth term premium. Auction dynamics confirm the bifurcation: T-bills cleared cleanly with bid-cover ratios of **1.92x** on the 91-day and **1.75x** on the 364-day with zero devolvement, while the 10-year bond managed only **1.4x** coverage and forced **10% devolvement** onto primary dealers — a telling sign that duration appetite beyond five years is thin and grudging.

The structural backdrop explains why the market is paralyzed between competing forces. Excess liquidity stands at a massive **৳1.9 lakh crore**, call money is trading at **9.8%** — comfortably within Bangladesh Bank's corridor — and the advance-to-deposit ratio at **75.8%** suggests banks are not credit-constrained. Under normal circumstances, this wall of liquidity should be compressing term premia and flattening the curve. It is not, and the reason is fiscal. Revenue collection at just **44.9%** of the FY26 target with the fiscal year-end barely three months away signals that the government will need to accelerate borrowing dramatically in Q4. ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%** means expenditure is back-loaded, and when it hits, the treasury pipeline will swell. Total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — **40% of GDP** — with interest-to-revenue already consuming **22%** creates a structural bid for higher yields on anything beyond the very short end. Meanwhile, CPI at **9.13%** with food inflation at **9.3%** leaves real yields barely positive on T-bills and arguably negative after tax on short tenors. The taka at **121.2** against the dollar with reserves at **$35.1 billion** provides a modest cushion, but Brent at **$78.4** and LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** keep the import bill elevated, constraining BB's room to ease even if growth considerations might warrant it. M2 growth of **9.5%** is running below nominal GDP growth, suggesting monetary conditions are tighter than the headline liquidity number implies — banks are hoarding, not deploying.

Here is the call for this week. The 2-year at **11.95%** is the most compelling value on this curve — it sits **195 basis points** above the repo rate, offers a meaningful pickup over the distorted belly, and its tenor is short enough to limit mark-to-market pain if the government's borrowing surge pushes long-end yields wider. I would be actively adding 2-year exposure and trimming the 15-year, which at **12.68%** appears rich relative to its illiquidity and the **103-basis-point** inversion it shows against the 20-year. The front-end T-bill book should be maintained but not extended; at **9.85%** on the 91-day, you are earning less than repo, and the only justification is SLR compliance. Avoid the 10-year until bid-cover ratios improve above **1.6x** — the **10% devolvement** on the last auction is a yellow flag that price discovery is incomplete at **11.15%**. The catalyst to watch is the March-end government borrowing calendar: if Bangladesh Bank announces an outsized April-June issuance slate to plug the revenue gap, the long end will reprice violently, and today's frozen yields will look like the calm before a storm. Conversely, if BB signals tolerance for monetary financing or loosens CRR from **4%**, the liquidity wall will crash into bonds and compress spreads faster than anyone positioned for it. Position for asymmetry — own the 2-year, hedge the tail, and keep powder dry for what April reveals.
